text: We present a video of a laparoscopic right colectomy following an ilio-orthotopic bladder substitute constructed close to twenty years ago. The challenges associated with performing a right colectomy after a Studer pouch include lysing adhesions, keeping the vascular supply of the pouch intact, avoiding injury to the pouch during dissection, and achieving an appropriate oncologic resection. There is a lack of published literature on these challenges. To perform a studer pouch, you would isolate an ileo segment, fifty-four to sixty centimeters long, approximately twenty-five centimeters proximal to the ileocecal valve. An ileo-ileo anastomosis is performed to restore bowel continuity. The ureters are then split and anastomosed to the proximal unopened part of the ileo segment. You then create an opening in the caudal part of the reservoir and this will be anastomosed to the urethra.

The patient is a seventy-seven-year-old male who underwent a diagnostic colonoscopy and was noted to have a large polyp in the right colon over the ileocecal valve extending into the terminal ileum. The patient's past history was significant for ilio-orthotopic bladder, also called a suitor pouch, constructed nearly seventeen years ago for bladder cancer. The biopsy of the polyp returned as tubulovilous adenoma and this was deemed to be endoscopically unresectable. He was referred for surgical intervention and a CT of the abdomen pelvis was obtained for operative planning. The CT scan showed thickening over the ileocecal valve consistent with the presence of the polyp. The ilioanastomosis and the segment of the ilium used for neobladder reconstruction can also be seen.

The patient was counseled regarding laparoscopic right colectomy with possibility of conversion to open. Urological consultation regarding placement of bilateral ureteral catheters through the neobladder was sought and deemed to be impossible. The urologist felt that placing stents into reimplanted ureters would be too difficult to find, cannulate, and pass stents into as they could easily coil. Also, neocystoscopy is challenging due to the altered anatomy.

The surgeon places a VisiPort in the left upper quadrant and removes adhesions between the omentum and abdominal wall, revealing multiple loops of small bowel adhered to the abdominal wall, particularly in the right lower quadrant. A 12mm port is inserted through a small incisional hernia defect above the umbilicus. The ileo-ileo anastomosis is visible, marked by anastomotic staples. Sharp dissection is used to separate the small bowel loops from the abdominal wall, avoiding energy devices near the bowel. The Studer pouch is identified by injecting saline diluted with methylene blue through a Foley catheter, making the blue dye visible through the pouch wall. The ileocolic junction and ileo-ileo anastomosis, located approximately 12 centimeters from the ileocecal valve, are observed. A 5mm port is placed in the right mid-abdomen to assist with dissection and retraction, and the surgeon dissects posterior to the ileo-ileo anastomosis, separating small bowel loops and exposing the posterior wall of the anastomosis.

The surgeon performs sharp dissection between loops of small bowel and the antireflux nipple valve. Due to adhesions obscuring the iliocolic pedicle, the colon is mobilized from lateral to medial, starting along the white line and proceeding to the hepatocolic ligament. The omentum is separated from the transverse colon, and the colon is mobilized until reaching the duodenum. The right ureter is visualized near the antireflux nipple valve. A window is created in the mesentery to isolate the ileocolic pedicle, which is then taken down using a harmonic energy device.

The surgeon assesses the perfusion of the Studer pouch and nipple valve using indocyanine green injection, confirming intact vascularization after the ileocolic pedicle is taken down. The ileo and colonic mesentery are divided with a harmonic scalpel. The specimen, including the ileoileal anastomosis and adjacent ileum, is externalized for extracorporeal anastomosis. Indocyanine green is injected again to confirm perfusion of the anastomosis, which is returned inside with confirmed hemostasis. The duodenum remains intact, and the Studer pouch and nipple valve are well vascularized.

No spillage of urine was noted during the operation. The patient made an uneventful recovery and was discharged on post-operative day two. He required replacement of the Foley catheter on post-op day one for urinary retention, which was later removed on post-op day seven. Final pathology showed a twelve centimeter tubulovilous adenoma over the ileocecal valve extending all the way up to the ilioilial anastomosis. The patient was last seen in the colorectal clinic two weeks postoperatively. He was progressing appropriately without any late ischemic injuries to the neobladder noted. In summary, our video demonstrates a laparoscopic right colectomy following an ilio-orthotopic neobladder.
